verses.commentary: Despite Israel's rejection of Christ, Paul longs for their salvation. Prayer flows from love. Israel needs salvation like everyone else.
Zeal without knowledge leads astray. Sincerity does not guarantee truth. Right intentions with wrong theology lead to destruction.
This is the essence of legalism. Submitting to God's righteousness means accepting Christ's righteousness by faith.
Christ is where the law was pointing. The law's purpose was to lead to Christ. Righteousness is now available to everyone who believes, not just those who keep the law.
Law righteousness requires perfect obedience. Do and live was the principle. But no one does perfectly, so no one lives by law-keeping.
It does not require impossible feats. Ascending to heaven to bring Christ down is unnecessary; Christ has already come. The incarnation makes this quest obsolete.
The resurrection is accomplished. Faith does not require heroic efforts.

But what does it say? The word is near you; it is in your mouth and in your heart (Deuteronomy 30:14), that is, the message concerning faith that we proclaim. The word of faith is accessible, not distant. It is near: in your mouth (confession) and in your heart (belief). The gospel is simple, not complicated.
Confession: Jesus is Lord (kyrios, κύριος, affirming His deity and authority). Belief: God raised Jesus. The resurrection is central. These together result in salvation.
Mouth confession leads to salvation. Belief and confession are inseparable. True faith confesses Christ publicly.
Believing in Christ guarantees no shame (no disappointment). This is the promise of faith. Trusting Christ is never regretted.
No distinction in salvation. Jew and Gentile come to God the same way. The same Lord (Jesus) is Lord of all. He richly blesses all who call on Him. Salvation is universally available through Christ.
Calling on the name of the Lord is the means of salvation. Everyone means no exceptions. This is the simplicity of the gospel: call on Jesus and be saved.
Calling requires belief. Belief requires hearing. Hearing requires preaching. The gospel must be proclaimed for people to be saved.
Preaching requires sending. Sent messengers are God's appointed means. Beautiful feet describe the messenger's precious role. Bringing good news is a glorious task.
The tragedy: not all who hear believe. Israel heard but did not accept. Belief is not automatic. Unbelief persists despite the message.
The content: the word about Christ. Faith is created by the proclaimed gospel. Hearing God's word produces faith.

Israel's problem was not lack of opportunity. They heard. The message reached them. The psalmist's words about creation's testimony are applied to gospel proclamation. The message has gone everywhere.
Israel did understand. Moses prophesied that Gentiles (those not a nation) would provoke Israel to jealousy. This has happened. Gentile salvation should move Israel to seek Christ.
The irony: Gentiles who did not seek God found Him. God revealed Himself to those who were not asking. Grace reaches the unexpected.

God's posture toward Israel: hands held out in invitation. Yet Israel is disobedient and obstinate. God's patience is met with rebellion. This is the tragedy: divine grace rejected by human stubbornness.
